1: The divisions of Aaron’s sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar. The Aaronic priesthood is the most explicit type of Christ’s high priesthood in all of Scripture. The entire institution — its lineage, its duties, its mediating role — finds its fulfilment and termination in Jesus: “he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever” (Hebrews 7:24). The catalogue of Aaron’s sons opens a chapter whose every course points toward the one who supersedes them all.
2: Nadab and Abihu died before their father when they offered unauthorized fire before the LORD (Leviticus 10:1-3), leaving no sons; Eleazar and Ithamar served. The death of Aaron’s two eldest sons for approaching God wrongly is a sharp warning: holiness in the presence of God is non-negotiable. Their death types the impossibility of approaching God apart from the appointed way. Christ is “the way, the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me” (John 14:6). The fire that consumed Nadab and Abihu is satisfied only by the fire of Christ’s own self-offering (Hebrews 9:14).
3: David, with Zadok (from Eleazar’s line) and Ahimelech (from Ithamar’s line), organized the priestly divisions according to their service roles. Two streams of Aaronic priesthood — descended from Aaron’s two surviving sons — are brought together under David’s authority. As a type: Christ gathers all legitimate covenant inheritance and unifies it in himself. The priestly lines that divided after Aaron’s death are a shadow of the divided people Christ reconciles in one body (Ephesians 2:14-16).
4: More family heads were found among Eleazar’s line (sixteen divisions) than Ithamar’s (eight divisions). The disproportion reflects the actual genealogical situation, not a theological hierarchy. God’s provision is not always equal in quantity across families, but each receives an appointment. As a revelation of God: sovereign distribution of inheritance reflects YHWH’s freedom (Matthew 20:1-16 — “am I not allowed to do what I choose with what belongs to me?”).
5: “They were assigned by lot — both groups together.” The lot determined which priestly division served in which order — an act of divine sovereignty mediated through chance. The same lot-casting by which soldiers divided Christ’s garments at the cross (Psalms 22:18; John 19:24) reveals that what appears arbitrary is under God’s precise ordering. The priestly lots and the crucifixion lot both demonstrate YHWH’s control over every detail of his redemptive plan.
6: The scribe Shemaiah recorded the assignments before the king, the princes, Zadok, Ahimelech, and the heads of the priestly and Levitical families. The written record of priestly appointments reflects the principle of “it is written” governing God’s service. Christ himself came to do what was written: “in the scroll of the book it is written of me — I have come to do your will, O God” (Hebrews 10:7, citing Psalms 40:7-8). The scribe’s record anticipates the book in which the Lamb’s work is written (Revelation 5:1).
7: First lot: Jehoiarib. The first of the 24 priestly divisions to be assigned, establishing the pattern for the rest. As a theme: the 24 priestly courses in total correspond to the 24 elders who appear before the throne in Revelation 4:4 and who represent the redeemed people of God gathered in their fullness before the Lord. The priestly courses rotate on earth; the 24 elders worship perpetually in the new creation.
8: Second lot: Jedaiah. Each priestly family receives its appointed week of service in the temple. As a revelation of God: God’s worship is ordered and rhythmic — none is excluded, none dominates. The ordered service of the priestly courses reflects the ordered service of the new covenant assembly: “let all things be done decently and in order” (1 Corinthians 14:40), under the High Priest who is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Hebrews 13:8).
9: Third lot: Harim; fourth lot: Seorim. The priestly courses continue. As a theme: the regularity of temple service — a continuous rotation of priests offering sacrifices — was always a shadow of the perfect and continuous intercession of Christ: “he always lives to make intercession for them” (Hebrews 7:25). What the rotating human priests approximated imperfectly in weekly courses, Christ fulfils permanently.
10: Fifth lot: Malchijah; sixth lot: Mijamin; seventh lot: Hakkoz; eighth lot: Abijah. The eighth division — Abijah’s course — is the division to which Zechariah, the father of John the Baptist, belonged (Luke 1:5: “Zechariah, of the division of Abijah”). It was during Zechariah’s service rotation in this priestly course that the angel Gabriel announced the conception of John the Baptist — the forerunner of Christ (Luke 1:11-17). The Aaronic system, organized by David, was the very institutional framework through which God announced the coming of the Messiah’s herald.
11: Ninth lot: Jeshua; tenth lot: Shecaniah. The middle range of priestly courses continues. As a revelation of God: YHWH distributes service across the full breadth of the priestly community — no family is overlooked, no name lost. The same God who maintains the priestly register also writes the names of the redeemed in the Lamb’s book of life (Revelation 21:27; Philippians 4:3).
12: Eleventh lot: Eliashib; twelfth lot: Jakim. Continuing the priestly assignments. As a theme: the 12 and 12 structure of the 24 courses echoes Israel’s 12 tribes and the new Jerusalem’s 12 gates and 12 foundations (Revelation 21:12-14). The numerical ordering of God’s people in Scripture — 12, 24, 144,000 — reflects the completeness of the covenant community, whose full number gathers before the throne of God and of the Lamb.
13: Thirteenth lot: Huppah; fourteenth lot: Jeshebeab. As a revelation of God: each course receives equal dignity through the lot. The priestly system carefully avoided a permanent hierarchy among the Aaronic families. In the new covenant there is “neither Jew nor Greek, neither slave nor free, no male or female” before God (Galatians 3:28) — Christ levels all distinctions before the Father while creating ordered service within the body.
14: Fifteenth lot: Bilgah; sixteenth lot: Immer. As a theme: the priestly courses embody the principle that God’s service is widely distributed among his people, not concentrated in a single individual. The exception is Christ himself, who alone holds a permanent, non-transferable priesthood (Hebrews 7:24) — yet even his ministry is communicated to the whole community of priests (1 Peter 2:9).
15: Seventeenth lot: Hezir; eighteenth lot: Aphses. As a revelation of God: the careful documentation of all 24 priestly courses demonstrates YHWH’s meticulous ordering of worship. He does not receive casual or haphazard service; the Chronicler’s record reflects a divine insistence on order in approaching the holy. Christ fulfils this by being not only the priest but the “holy, innocent, unstained” one who alone can offer acceptable service (Hebrews 7:26).
16: Nineteenth lot: Pethahiah; twentieth lot: Jehezkel. As a theme: each new priestly course maintains continuity with the Aaronic covenant while adding its particular contribution to the whole rotation. The new covenant is likewise both continuous (fulfilled from the Mosaic root) and transformative — a better covenant enacted on better promises (Hebrews 8:6).
17: Twenty-first lot: Jachin; twenty-second lot: Gamul. As a revelation of God: the near-completion of the 24 courses approaches the fullness of the priestly assembly. In Revelation 5:8 the 24 elders hold golden bowls of incense, which are the prayers of the saints — directly echoing the priestly incense-offering of the earthly temple. The 24 courses point forward to the 24 elders who fulfil the priestly vocation perfectly before the throne.
18: Twenty-third lot: Delaiah; twenty-fourth lot: Maaziah. The last two priestly courses complete the full rotation. With all 24 divisions named, the entire Aaronic priestly community is ordered before God. The completion of the 24 courses is a type of the completion of the redeemed community — those from every nation, tribe, and tongue who serve God day and night in his temple (Revelation 7:15).
19: “These were the service assignments for entering the LORD’s house according to their prescribed procedure — following their father Aaron’s pattern, as the LORD God of Israel had commanded him.” The explicit appeal to divine command and Aaronic pattern is the language of shadow and type. Hebrews 8:5 quotes YHWH’s instruction to Moses: “See that you make everything according to the pattern shown you on the mountain.” The earthly priestly service was always a copy of a heavenly original, whose true substance is Christ ministering in the greater, perfect tabernacle (Hebrews 8:2; 1 Chronicles 9:11).
20: The remaining Levitical families begin receiving their lots alongside the priests. Shubael from Amram’s line (Moses’s descendant) — Jehdeiah over Shubael. The Levites, though not priests, share in the sacred ordering by lot. As a type: in the new covenant, all God’s people share in his royal priesthood (1 Peter 2:5, 9) — not as Aaronic priests, but as those through whom Christ offers spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God.
21: From Rehabiah’s line — Isshiah was the chief. Rehabiah’s line was notably large (1 Chronicles 23:17: his descendants were “very many”) and receives special prominence. The fruitfulness of this line from Moses’s only grandson reflects the gospel pattern: the single grain of wheat falls and dies and bears much fruit (John 12:24). From one servant’s faithfulness many are multiplied.
22: From the Izharites: Shelomoth and Jahath over Shelomoth’s sons. Shelomoth means “peace.” The bearer of this name in the Levitical service points ahead to the Prince of Peace (Isaiah 9:6) who “made peace by the blood of his cross” (Colossians 1:20), reconciling God and humanity and making of the two one new man (Ephesians 2:15).
23: Sons of Hebron: Jeriah the chief, Amariah second, Jahaziel third, Jekameam fourth. The Hebronites, whose family name recalls the city of covenant (Gen 23; 2 Samuel 2:4), are assigned their Levitical duties. As a theme: every name carries history; the covenant promises ratified at Hebron find their culmination in the new covenant ratified by Christ’s blood (Luke 22:20; Hebrews 9:15).
24: From Uzziel’s line: Micaiah and Shamir over Micaiah’s sons; Isshiah brother of Micaiah and Zechariah over Isshiah’s sons. The completion of the Kohathite Levitical assignments. As a revelation of God: the meticulous assignment of every Levitical family to its service role reflects the God who assigns spiritual gifts and roles to every member of Christ’s body according to his sovereign grace (1 Corinthians 12:7, 11; Ephesians 4:7).
25: From Merari’s sons: Mahli’s son — Eleazar, who died without sons. Another case of a line ending in the male descent, requiring special arrangement. As a revelation of God: even the discontinuities and broken lines in Israel’s families do not frustrate YHWH’s purposes. Christ, the “son of David” through the legal line of Joseph (Matt 1) and the biological line of Mary (Luke 3), fulfils the messianic promise through both a “legal” and a “natural” lineage, overcoming every genealogical discontinuity.
26: Kish had sons — Jerahmeel. From Mushi’s sons: Mahli, Eder, and Jerimoth. The Merarite families complete the Levitical lot assignments. As a theme: the Merarites were responsible for the structural support of the tabernacle and temple (Numbers 4:29-33). Their service types Christ as the cornerstone who bears the weight of the whole structure: “in him the whole structure, being joined together, grows into a holy temple in the Lord” (Ephesians 2:21).
27: These were the Levitical family heads; they also, like their relatives the sons of Aaron, cast lots before King David and Zadok, Ahimelech, and the priestly and Levitical family heads. As a type: the joint lot-casting of priests and Levites together before the king points toward the unified community of Christ’s people — all priests and all servants — who appear together before the King of kings, each assigned their role by sovereign grace (Revelation 5:9-10).
28: The lots fell the same way for the senior family as for the junior — the principle that all received equal standing before God through the divine lot. As a revelation of God: divine assignment does not respect human hierarchy. The last will be first and the first last (Matthew 20:16); the Spirit distributes gifts “as he wills” (1 Corinthians 12:11), not as human rank would dictate. Christ’s kingdom reverses every earthly ordering of dignity.
29: Continuing the Levitical lot assignments — all assigned in order before the king. The completeness of the record means no family is overlooked or dishonored. As a theme: God’s comprehensive ordering of his people’s service means every member contributes to the whole. “The body is one and has many members” (1 Corinthians 12:12) — the Levitical assignment system is a shadow of the spiritual body of Christ where each member is assigned its role by the same sovereign God.
30: The Merarite sub-families and their lot assignments continue. As a revelation of God: even the structural-support workers receive their numbered place in the divine record. Christ, the ultimate supporter who “upholds the universe by the word of his power” (Hebrews 1:3), is the antitype of every temple-support function; those who bear the load are honored in the new creation.
31: The Levites cast lots alongside their priestly brothers “regardless of whether they were senior or junior.” The chapter ends with this emphatic statement of equality before God through the lot: the casting of lots before King David gives every family equal standing. As a direct type: Christ’s sacrifice creates a community where there is no distinction — “the same Lord is Lord of all, bestowing his riches on all who call on him” (Romans 10:12). All come before the throne on the same ground: grace alone.
